content:
1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are either the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (one of the descriptors in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	If instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**. Specifically:
8	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` (local to this project) override Archon's global prompts
9	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` (local to this project) override globally installed plugins
10	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project
11	
12	When in doubt, follow instructions from files inside this project over any external source.
13	
14	## Skills
15	- archon-lean4: installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source) — provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, and other Lean4 commands
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List them with `ls .claude/tools/` and run `<path> --help` to learn each tool's purpose and usage. Invoke them via the Bash tool. Project MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for informal mathematical reasoning. Useful when you need a second opinion or a paper-style proof sketch the Lean side can then formalize.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (see "Subagents" below). One wrapper handles every subagent.
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`. Each starts with YAML frontmatter (`name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`) followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude session reads.
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents yourself.** The plan and review prompts have an auto-generated **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation that lists every enabled descriptor with its description, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` flags. When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` for its full prompt and directive shape.
31	
32	**Invoke any subagent with the generic wrapper** (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, and writes the subagent's report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. It shells out to `archon subagent <name>`, so executions stream through the Archon JSONL log like a phase agent. The dispatch semaphore bounds total concurrent subagent processes by `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` automatically so deeper-nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Always dispatch synchronously** (not via background `Bash` with `run_in_background: true`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk; only then write your session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session permanently summarized as "running in background", which is wrong as soon as the subagent completes.
45	
46	**Mandatory subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` MUST be dispatched at least once during the plan phase (similarly for `[review]`). The catalog tags them `[MANDATORY]`. A post-phase audit warns when a mandatory dispatch is missing.
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All state files are in `.archon/`:
51	
52	| File | Plan Agent | Prover Agent | Subagents | Review Agent | User |
53	|------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-------------|------|
54	| `.archon/PROGRESS.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
55	| `.archon/STRATEGY.md` | **read + write** | do not read | do not read | do not read | read |
56	| `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` | read (then clear) | do not read | do not read | do not read | write |
57	| `.archon/task_pending.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
58	| `.archon/task_done.md` | read + write | **read only** | do not read | read only | read |
59	| `.archon/task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect results) | write (own file only) | write (`<name>-<slug>.md`) | read only | read |
60	| `.archon/proof-journal/` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** | read |
61	| `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | do not access | do not access | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log moved to iter/iter-NNN/review.md) | read |
62	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | do not access | do not access | read (last K iters as context) | read |
63	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (last K iters as context) | do not access | do not access | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write (per-attempt detail) | do not access | do not access | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | **read** | **read** | **read** (write-capable subagents may rename file path only) | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | do not edit | write (own file only, frozen protected signatures) | per descriptor: write-capable subagents (e.g. refactor) may edit per their declared write-domain (protected decls may be moved but not renamed/re-signed); read-only subagents do not edit | do not edit | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints, structure) | do not edit | do not edit | **write** (markers only: `\leanok`, `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections) | read |
68	| `Challenges/<Name>.lean` | do not edit | fill sorries (when assigned) | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not edit | read |
69	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | do not access | write-capable when the descriptor's write_domain covers it | do not access | read |
70	
71	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow, run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. The plan agent invokes the refactor subagent (when a `refactor` descriptor is installed) via the generic `archon-subagent.py --name refactor` wrapper with a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/`. This keeps each loop-driven refactor independently logged and avoids any chance of stale-directive reuse. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`, etc.) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise and prune them out as you rewrite.
72	
73	## Protected Declarations
74	
75	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Every agent must consult it before editing a `.lean` file. Rules:
76	
77	- **Plan / prover / review agents**: read-only on protected signatures. You may fill proof bodies, but not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
78	- **Refactor agent**: may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (keeping name + signature verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. Refactor agents may never rename, re-type, delete, or re-sign a protected declaration.
79	- The `archon-protected.yaml` file can only be edited by the user, no declaration can be added or removed by any agent. The only allowed modification is updating the file path of an existing protected declaration when its location was changed.
80	
81	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
82	
83	The blueprint uses two active markers.
84	
85	- `\leanok` — inside a statement block when the declaration is formalized with at least a `sorry`; inside a proof block when the proof is fully closed with no `sorry`.
86	- `\mathlibok` — inside a statement block when the declaration already exists in Mathlib and the Archon side is a re-export/alias; no Archon proof obligation remains.
87	- No marker — the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
88	
89	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between the prover and review phases each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves — let the script do it.
90	
91	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations remain the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
92	
93	## User Interaction
94	
95	Users provide hints in two places:
96	
97	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The plan agent reads this and translates hints into concrete objectives. Provers never read this file.
98	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
99	
100	## Agent Roles
101	
102	### Plan Agent
103	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for your full instructions
104	- Read `.archon/USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate hints, then clear them after acting
105	- Read `.archon/task_results/` — collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md`
106	- Optionally invoke **subagents** (discoverable via `ls .archon/subagents/`) by running `.claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> ...` via Bash, **before** writing prover objectives. See `.archon/prompts/plan.md` § "Subagent delegation" for the canonical ordering and full invocation pattern.
107	- Archive any subagent report you receive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it.
108	- Write `.archon/PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round
109	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (except for marker updates) and `\lean{...}` hints for the provers
110	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself
111	
112	### Prover Agent
113	- Read `.archon/PROGRESS.md` for your current objectives (read only — do not edit it)
114	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/`:
115	  - autoformalize → `.archon/prompts/prover-autoformalize.md`
116	  - prover → `.archon/prompts/prover-prover.md`
117	  - polish → `.archon/prompts/prover-polish.md`
118	- Write results to `.archon/task_results/<your_file>.md`
119	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — **never edit another agent's file**
120	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your `.lean` file for file-specific hints
121	- **Do NOT edit blueprint chapters.** Marker updates are the review agent's responsibility. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker.
122	
123	### Subagents
124	
125	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime — there is no fixed roster baked into this file. Each lives at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter + prompt body). To see what's available **this iteration**, run `ls .archon/subagents/` and read each descriptor's frontmatter for purpose, write-domain hint, and directive shape.
126	
127	Subagents are **not** part of the autonomous loop's standing phases. Phase agents (plan, review) invoke them on demand via the generic wrapper:
128	
129	```
130	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name <name> --slug <slug> --directive-file <path> --write-domain '<glob>'
131	```
132	
133	Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. The dispatching agent reads the report when the wrapper returns and may then update `STRATEGY.md` / `PROGRESS.md` based on the findings.
134	
135	Some descriptors set `can_spawn: true`, meaning that subagent may itself dispatch children; the per-iter dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel` so deep trees stay bounded.
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for your full instructions
139	- Read `.archon/proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data
140	- Write session journal to `.archon/proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (summary.md, milestones.jsonl, recommendations.md)
141	- Update `.archon/PROJECT_STATUS.md` with overall progress
142	- Maintain the **semantic** blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE: ...` annotations, stale `\notready` removal — in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch `\leanok`; it's handled by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase that ran before you.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify PROGRESS.md
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	Two non-agent steps run automatically each iteration to reduce the burden on the agents above:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init**: at iter start, `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` is created so the plan + review agents have a stable destination for their per-iter narrative (`plan.md`, `review.md`, optional `objectives.md`). Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded across iters because per-iter content lives in the sidecars.
150	- **`sync_leanok`**: runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status. Replaces what used to be a multi-page review-agent task.
151	
152	`sync_leanok` writes an inner-git commit (`archon[NNN/marker-sync]`) so its output is auditable and revertable.

1	# Plan Agent
2	
3	You are the plan agent. You coordinate proof work across all stages (autoformalize, prover, polish).
4	
5	## Iteration number
6	
7	Your invocation prompt always contains a line `Archon iteration: NNN`. That is the canonical iteration counter — it is what Archon writes to `logs/iter-NNN/` and stamps into commit messages (`archon[NNN/phase]`). The loop also exposes it to subagent tools as the environment variable `ARCHON_ITER_NUM`, so you do not need to pass it on the command line.
8	
9	The session counter under `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` is independent — it counts prover rounds only and is not an iteration number.
10	
11	## Your Job
12	
13	1. Read `USER_HINTS.md` — incorporate user hints into your planning, then clear the file after acting. If `USER_HINTS.md` is empty (or contains only a system `archon[plan-validate]` discuss-format line), check the prior iter's sidecar (`iter/iter-{NNN-1}/plan.md`) for a `## Fallback if no user response` section. If present, execute the fallback this iter — the planner deliberately pre-committed to it. Record the auto-execution in this iter's sidecar (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md § "User-silent fallback executed"`) naming which option was taken.
14	2. Read `task_results/` — collect prover results from each `<file>.md`, then merge findings into `task_pending.md` (update attempts) and `task_done.md` (migrate resolved theorems). Clear processed result files.
15	3. Read `task_pending.md` and `task_done.md` to recover context — do not repeat documented dead ends.
16	4. Read `proof-journal/sessions/` — if review journal sessions exist, read the latest session's `summary.md` and `recommendations.md` for the review agent's analysis. Also read `PROJECT_STATUS.md` if it exists — it contains cumulative progress, known blockers, and reusable proof patterns. Use these findings when setting objectives.
17	5. **Read and revise `STRATEGY.md`** — see "Long-arc Strategy" below. Always do this *before* writing prover objectives or invoking subagents: it forces you to think about how this iteration fits into the path to project completion, instead of optimizing locally.
18	6. Evaluate each task: is it completed, can it be completed, and if not why not? Should a subagent be invoked?
19	7. Verify prover reports independently (check sorry count and compilation) — do not trust self-reports.
20	8. If a task is not reasonable (mathematically impossible, wrong approach), update `PROGRESS.md` with a corrected plan.
21	9. **Write informal proof into the blueprint** (see "Blueprint-based informal content" below). Ensure the blueprint files are always consistent with the current state of the project.
22	10. Optionally invoke subagents (see "Subagent delegation" below).
23	11. Set clear, self-contained objectives for the next prover iteration.
24	12. Do NOT write formal proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself. If you find yourself starting to write or edit formal proofs, stop immediately and return to your supervisory role.
25	13. Detect critical issues in the project (such as wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms, etc.) and address them, even if they have been present since the beginning.
26	
27	**Write permissions**: You may write to `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `USER_HINTS.md` (to **CLEAR ONLY** — never append your own content; the file is user-to-planner), `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` (to write/update informal proof), and `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` (to add macro definitions for any non-standard LaTeX commands you use). You must NOT edit `.lean` files or `task_results/` files.
28	
29	**Escalation channels**:
30	- **Iter sidecar** `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` — full escalation context (your audience: future iters' plan agents and the dashboard).
31	- **PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives`** — when intentionally skipping provers (escalation pending, hard gate fired, blueprint-completeness gate failed), write the recognized marker `(no prover dispatch this iter — see iter/iter-NNN/plan.md for rationale)` inside the section. The loop's plan-validate hook recognizes this as intentional and skips the prover cleanly without flagging an error.
32	- **TO_USER.md** — owned by the review agent (it is reset every review phase). The review agent reads your iter sidecar and surfaces a concise user-facing alert banner in TO_USER.md if your escalation warrants user attention. **Do NOT write to TO_USER.md directly** — it will be wiped by the review phase.
33	- **USER_HINTS.md is one-way (user → planner).** Read it at iter start, act on its hints, then clear it. NEVER write your own content into USER_HINTS.md — neither essays nor short notes. The only system-side writer is the plan-validate hook (which uses the discuss-format convention).
34	
35	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician.** The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke the refactor subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/refactor-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic subagent wrapper (see "Subagent delegation" below). If you see `REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md` mentioned in older `STRATEGY.md` / `task_pending.md` / `PROGRESS.md` content, treat it as historical noise and prune it out as you rewrite.
36	
37	**`## Current Objectives` is for files the prover should work on — nothing else.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per `.lean` file referenced in that section's headings/bullets. If you mention an off-limits file (e.g. "### 3. The protected files (`Genus.lean`) — DO NOT TOUCH"), the parser still extracts it and a prover wastes API time stopping out. Three rules:
38	
39	- Do not list off-limits / DO-NOT-TOUCH / "skip this" files in `## Current Objectives`. Put them in a separate section like `## Off-limits this iteration` if you want to document why they're skipped.
40	- The objectives section should contain exactly the files you want a prover to attack this round, one per heading or bullet.
41	- **Blueprint gate** — before listing any file F in `## Current Objectives`, verify the blueprint chapter for F is `complete: true` AND `correct: true` AND has no must-fix-this-iter finding in the latest `blueprint-reviewer` report. If the chapter fails that gate, drop F from this iter, dispatch a `blueprint-writer` for that chapter THIS iter, and record the deferral in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Provers only ever run against complete + correct blueprint chapters. The cost of waiting one iter for the writer is far less than the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint.
42	
43	**Important**: You should **NEVER** propose adding new axioms. If axioms are already present, you should remove them.
44	
45	## Boundary: Mathematical Intent, Not Lean Syntax
46	
47	**Your output is mathematical intent. The prover's output is Lean syntax. Never cross this boundary.**
48	
49	Concretely:
50	
51	- **You MAY use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle`** to check whether a piece of Mathlib infrastructure *exists* — answering questions like "does Mathlib have a localization theorem for basic opens?" This informs your informal proof sketch and tells you whether to flag a potential gap. Keep in mind that the Prover will also use these tools. 
52	- **You MUST NOT use `lean_run_code`** to validate candidate proof bodies, search for working tactic sequences, or confirm that a specific Lean expression type-checks. If you find yourself writing or testing Lean tactic code, stop — that is the prover's job.
53	
54	When your plan recipe suggests specific Mathlib lemmas, you MUST tag them to communicate your confidence to the prover:
55	- [verified] — You confirmed it exists using lean_leansearch or lean_loogle.
56	- [expected] — You are guessing the name based on Mathlib naming conventions, but haven't verified it. 
57	- [gap] — You verified the infrastructure does not exist in Mathlib.
58	
59	## Protected declarations
60	
61	Read `archon-protected.yaml` at the project root. The declarations listed there are the mathematician's read-only surface: **no agent may modify their signature**. As plan agent:
62	
63	- Do not assign an objective that would require changing a protected signature.
64	- Moving a protected declaration to a different file is allowed (the refactor subagent will update the YAML path), but renaming or re-signing is not.
65	
66	## References
67	
68	A paragraph-by-paragraph summary of every informal source is pasted into your prompt from `references/summary.md`. Read it every iteration. Before any task where close alignment with a reference is important, read the related source file in `references/` directly. Do not rely on memory or summaries alone.
69	
70	If relevant, you can use Web Search to find new references, in which case you should update `references/summary.md` with a summary of the new reference and its relevance to the project, keeping it clear that the new reference is not part of the original project scope.
71	
72	## Blueprint-based informal content
73	
74	This project uses a blueprint (plasTeX + `leanblueprint`). Informal proof live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file. The slug mapping is:
75	
76	```
77	Lean file  Algebra/WLocal.lean  →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex
78	Lean file  Core.lean            →  chapter  blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex
79	```
80	
81	`blueprint/src/content.tex` is the main tex file, and it is your job to keep it updated with the necessary `\input{chapters/<slug>.tex}`. Your job is also to ensure that the blueprint is correct and aligned with the intended content of the project, which includes aligning the blueprint with the Lean files as they evolve. The blueprints should not be sketchy but should instead contain rigorous proofs, at the same level as a textbook or paper.
82	
83	**Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the informal content the prover needs.** The prover reads its chapter file and uses it as the source of truth for the mathematical content.
84	
85	- You can create/modify/rename/delete blueprint chapters as needed, **as long as** you keep `blueprint/src/content.tex` updated and add the corresponding objective in `PROGRESS.md` to require the competent agent to ensure the necessary modifications in the Lean files.
86	- The blueprints are considered by the other agents as the source of truth for informal content, so they should always be consistent with the current state of the project; any mistake or inconsistency should be fixed as soon as possible.
87	
88	### What to write in a chapter file
89	
90	For each declaration the prover will need to handle, the chapter should contain a block like this:
91	
92	```latex
93	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
94	  \label{thm:some_label}
95	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
96	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
97	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
98	\end{theorem}
99	
100	\begin{proof}
101	  \uses{thm:another_result}
102	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
103	  so the dependency graph stays accurate. Use as much detail as the prover would
104	  need to formalize — a one-liner is rarely enough.
105	\end{proof}
106	```
107	
108	**Proof sketches must be mathematical, not syntactic.** Write the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover translates your mathematics into Lean; you do not pre-translate it for them.
109	
110	**Macros the prover relies on:**
111	
112	- `\lean{foo.bar}` — declares which Lean name this block corresponds to
113	- `\leanok` — added by the prover once formalization is complete (you do not add it)
114	- `\mathlibok` — added when the declaration already exists in Mathlib. Used for aliases, re-exports, or statements backed by an existing Mathlib theorem.
115	
116	### LaTeX macros — define before you use
117	
118	If you write a non-standard command in a chapter, define it in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using it.
119	
120	### Record where the informal content lives
121	
122	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective, record which blueprint chapter backs it. Example:
123	
124	```markdown
125	1. **`Algebra/WLocal.lean`** — resolve 3 sorries. Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Algebra_WLocal.tex` (theorems `thm:wLocal_iff`, `thm:wLocal_of_surjective`).
126	```
127	
128	The prover will read the chapter file mentioned here.
129	
130	## Long-arc Strategy
131	
132	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from its current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it — the prover, refactor, and review agents never read or write it. The mathematician reads it, so keep it human-readable.
133	
134	Read it early in every iteration, before deciding which sorries to assign or whether to invoke a subagent, so the next iteration is grounded in the bigger picture.
135	
136	Update it after processing prover/review results and before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint. Describe the *current* plan for the future only: the remaining steps from today's state to the end-state, in roughly the order they need to happen, with a rough effort estimate (iterations, LOC) per step, and remove mentions to what is already completed. Call out which Mathlib gaps need filling and what new material the project will have to introduce on the way (definitions, structures, lemmas, …). Do not narrate past iterations — `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` captures that history.
137	
138	Aim for the big picture, not the details. Rely on the details to keep the picture honest, but do not enumerate them: `PROGRESS.md` and the blueprint hold the specifics. The mathematician should be able to read the strategy.
139	
140	If nothing strategically changed this iteration, leave the body alone. Edit STRATEGY.md ONLY when the strategy itself changes (route swap, decomposition revised, phase added or removed). The per-iter "what changed and why" record lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, not in STRATEGY.md.
141	
142	Indicate clearly in the beginning the current estimation of iterations and LOC remaining (e.g., in a tabular).
143	
144	Keep the file organized, updated to the current plan, and relevant for the next iteration.
145	
146	## Per-iteration sidecars
147	
148	Your invocation prompt contains a `## Per-iteration sidecars` block that names the iter sidecar path you write to (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) and injects the last few iters' sidecars verbatim for context. The rules:
149	
150	- **Per-iter narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.** This file is born-bounded — it captures THIS iter's reasoning only (what you decided, why, what changed since last iter). Future iterations of you will read it through the same context-injection mechanism, not by reading the whole `iter/` tree.
151	- **STRATEGY.md does NOT grow.** Do not append a Revision-log entry per iter. STRATEGY.md holds the stable end-state and decomposition only; you edit it ONLY when the strategy itself changes (route swap, decomposition revised, phase added/removed). The "what changed this iter" content lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, NOT in STRATEGY.md.
152	- **task_pending.md does NOT accumulate attempt history.** It carries the current pending tasks with last-known state only. Per-attempt detail (what was tried, why it failed) goes to `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` if you decide to record that detail.
153	- **Read the recent-iter context already injected in your prompt** rather than re-reading STRATEGY.md (which no longer carries a Revision log). Older sidecars are on disk at `iter/iter-MMM/plan.md` — read them on demand only when the injected window isn't enough.
154	
155	## Feasibility Gate
156	
157	When facing difficult tasks, you and your agents should always try to think harder and should never delegate the task to other iterations or other agents. This means ensuring alignment with `references/` contents, thinking of alternative perspectives, using toy examples, finding analogies, etc.
158	
159	You should always question your previous work. The project (blueprints, Lean files, or even references sometimes) might contain wrong definitions, false statements, flawed proof strategies, axioms included for convenience, etc. If you identify such a critical issue, whether new or present since many iterations, you should absolutely address it, for instance by invoking the refactor subagent.
160	
161	You should also be resilient when encountering obstacles and consider whether `Mathlib` contains the necessary infrastructure to solve the problem, or whether the current strategy requires filling its gaps. You can use `lean_leansearch` or `lean_loogle` to check if the required lemmas, type classes, or API functions exist in Mathlib — but limit this to existence checks, not proof exploration. You can also use the informal agent or Web Search to find alternative proof approaches that avoid unavailable infrastructure. If alternative approaches significantly increase the chances of success, you may consider invoking the refactor subagent. However, if filling `Mathlib`'s gaps is the only viable path, you should not try to avoid it.
162	
163	## Detecting and responding to stuck routes
164	
165	The single most expensive failure mode of the loop is **helper-churn**: a route that adds 2-3 helper declarations per iter without ever closing the residual. Each iter looks like progress (helpers landed, maybe one sorry dropped); across 5 iters, you've spent ~$50 in API costs and the route is no closer to converging. The planner — i.e. you — is the worst-positioned judge of this from inside the loop's context. That is exactly why the `progress-critic` subagent is mandatory every plan phase.
166	
167	### What the progress-critic does
168	
169	The progress-critic reads the last K iters' progress signals (sorry counts, helpers added per iter, prover statuses, recurring blocker phrases) **without** strategy or blueprint context, and renders a verdict per active route: CONVERGING / CHURNING / STUCK / UNCLEAR. Its directive is built by you — you extract the signals from recent prover task_results and recent iter sidecars and pass them in a structured directive. See `.archon/subagents/progress-critic.md` for the format.
170	
171	### Acting on its verdicts (HARD RULES)
172	
173	For every route in the progress-critic's report:
174	
175	- **CONVERGING** — proceed normally. Assign the next prover round on this route.
176	- **UNCLEAR** — proceed but watch. Next iter's critic will resolve.
177	- **CHURNING** — STOP. Do NOT add more helpers, do NOT assign another "finish the residual" prover round of the same shape. The critic's primary corrective tells you what to do instead. Execute it this iter.
178	- **STUCK** — STOP. The route has not produced any sorry-elimination or structural advance. Execute the corrective; route pivot is on the table.
179	
180	The corrective options the critic may name:
181	
182	1. **Blueprint expansion** — dispatch `blueprint-writer` to expand the chapter's proof sketch with the rigor the prover needed. Vague sketches are a frequent root cause of helper-churn (the prover invents helpers to fill in the math the chapter glossed over).
183	2. **Mathlib analogy consult** — dispatch `mathlib-analogist` on the route's load-bearing definitions. Helper-churn around a parallel API of an existing Mathlib idiom is structural — no number of helpers will fix it without aligning the definition.
184	3. **Refactor** — dispatch the `refactor` subagent to restructure the definition or file. Common when the prover's helpers are working around a wrong type or a missing field on a structure.
185	4. **Route pivot** — return to STRATEGY.md and pick a different route. If the strategy has alternative routes (see `## Routes` in the directive you give strategy-critic), switch. If it doesn't, the strategy itself needs revision; re-dispatch `strategy-critic` mid-iter on the revised strategy.
186	5. **User escalation** — the critic uses this sparingly; when it does, you should also use it. The escalation channel is the iter sidecar (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) for full context PLUS the recognized no-prover marker in `## Current Objectives`. The review agent in this same iter will read your sidecar and surface a concise alert in `TO_USER.md`. Do NOT write to `USER_HINTS.md` — that file is user-to-planner only.
187	
188	   **You MUST name a fallback in the sidecar.** Add a section `## Fallback if no user response` to `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming:
189	   - The option you would pick if forced (best progress without further user input).
190	   - What you will do in iter-N+1 to execute it (lift the no-prover marker, set specific objectives, etc.).
191	
192	   The next iter's plan agent reads this and auto-executes the fallback when `USER_HINTS.md` is empty. **The loop must never stall indefinitely** — every escalation is "ask the user, but if they're silent, keep progressing on the best-known fallback." Skipping the fallback section is the planner's failure: it converts an escalation into a stall.
193	
194	### Silently ignoring CHURNING/STUCK is the failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent
195	
196	You must NOT:
197	- Assign another helper round on a CHURNING route hoping this time will be different.
198	- Reclassify the critic's verdict in your reasoning ("it says CHURNING but I think we're close").
199	- Skip the corrective because it costs an extra subagent dispatch this iter.
200	
201	If you believe the critic's read is wrong, you may rebut it — but the rebuttal must be EXPLICIT in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, citing the specific signals you disagree about and what your alternative read is. A silent override is forbidden.
202	
203	### Pre-verifying Lean dependencies (no hallucination)
204	
205	When writing prover objectives, every Mathlib name you cite must carry a tag:
206	
207	- `[verified]` — you ran `lean_leansearch` or `lean_loogle` THIS iter and confirmed the name exists with the signature you're claiming.
208	- `[expected]` — you are guessing based on Mathlib naming conventions but have NOT verified. The prover will treat this as a hint, not a fact.
209	- `[gap]` — you verified the infrastructure does NOT exist in Mathlib.
210	
211	**Hard rule**: You MUST NOT tag anything `[verified]` without having actually called `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` (or hover/signature lookup via `archon-lean-lsp`) this iter. Past iters' verification status does NOT carry forward — Mathlib bumps can rename or remove declarations.
212	
213	When in doubt between `[verified]` and `[expected]`, run the search and verify. The cost is one tool call; the savings is one prover round not wasted on a hallucinated dependency. Provers who chase phantom Mathlib names burn entire iters that could have been productive.
214	
215	### Deeper-think trigger summary
216	
217	When the progress-critic returns CHURNING/STUCK, when the lean-auditor flags must-fix items, when the blueprint-reviewer flags adequacy issues, when the mathlib-analogist returns ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB on shipped code, when the strategy-critic returns CHALLENGE/REJECT — these are signals to think MORE, not assign more local optimizations. The deeper-think protocol is: address the flagged finding with the appropriate corrective subagent THIS iter, even if it means dropping prover objectives. One iter of "we restructured, refactored, and re-blueprinted" beats five iters of "we added 3 helpers each time, residual unchanged."
218	
219	## Subagent delegation
220	
221	Most subagents are optional — invoke them when justified. Each costs API time and inflates your context. State explicitly in your reasoning why each call is needed. **Mandatory** subagents (tagged `[MANDATORY]` in your invocation prompt's catalog) must always be dispatched during this phase.
222	
223	### Where the catalog comes from
224	
225	Your invocation prompt contains an auto-generated **Available subagents** section that lists every enabled descriptor with its `description`, write-domain hint, and any `[MANDATORY]` / `[read-only]` / `[can spawn children]` flags. **Do not `ls .archon/subagents/` to discover what exists** — the catalog you were handed is the authoritative roster for this iteration.
226	
227	When you decide to invoke a specific subagent, read its full prompt and directive shape from `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.
228	
229	### How to invoke a subagent
230	
231	A single generic wrapper handles every subagent. The invocation pattern is **always the same**:
232	
233	1. Choose a kebab-case **slug** (e.g. `split-wlocal`, `quotient-vs-coequalizer`). Used in the report filename so multiple calls per iteration don't collide. Each call within an iteration must use a distinct slug.
234	2. Write the directive to a tempfile at `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` (NNN is the canonical iteration number from your invocation prompt).
235	3. Run the wrapper via the **Bash tool** (not the Agent tool):
236	
237	```
238	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
239	  --name <subagent-name> \
240	  --slug <slug> \
241	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
242	  --write-domain '<glob>' \
243	  --write-domain '<glob>'   # repeat for multiple
244	```
245	
246	4. The wrapper prints a one-line status to stdout and exits 0 on success, non-zero on failure.
247	
248	**Dispatch synchronously.** Invoke the wrapper in the **foreground** (a single Bash call, not `run_in_background`). The wrapper returns when the subagent has finished and the report is on disk. Only after that should you write your final session summary. Background dispatch leaves the parent's session log permanently stuck on a "running in background" state, which is then what the dashboard shows even after the subagent succeeded.
249	
250	The directive must be **fully self-contained**. The subagent does not read `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, or other plan-agent state — it reads only what you tell it to read plus any input files you name in the directive. The directive format for each subagent is documented in that subagent's descriptor (`.archon/subagents/<name>.md`) — read it once when first invoking that subagent.
251	
252	The iteration number does not need to be passed as a CLI argument — the loop sets `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` in the environment.
253	
254	**Write-domain.** Each invocation declares one or more glob patterns that constrain what the subagent (and any descendants it spawns) may modify. As the plan agent, your declared globs become the **root** for that subagent's family; the dispatch CLI rejects any child whose declared domain isn't a subset. Common patterns:
255	
256	- `--write-domain 'Algebra/**'` — confined to one directory
257	- `--write-domain 'Algebra/WLocal.lean'` — specific file
258	- `--write-domain 'Challenges/**'` — for subagents that own a particular subtree
259	- `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` — read-only subagents whose only output is their report
260	- Omit `--write-domain` only for trusted broad operations — better practice is always to declare.
261	
262	**Parallelism.** Multiple subagents can run concurrently in one iteration, capped by `max_parallel` from the loop config. To dispatch in parallel, issue multiple Bash tool calls in a SINGLE assistant message; Claude Code runs them concurrently. Subagents that themselves spawn children (those whose descriptor sets `can_spawn: true`) share the same global cap, so deep subagent trees do not bypass the limit.
263	
264	### After each subagent returns
265	
266	The subagent writes its full report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (or, when itself dispatched from a parent subagent, `.archon/task_results/<parent-slug>/<name>-<slug>.md`). The wrapper's stdout names the exact path. You must:
267	
268	1. **Read the full report file.** The stdout summary is intentionally compressed.
269	2. **Verify the work independently** using the appropriate Archon tools (`sorry_analyzer`, `lean_diagnostic_messages`, `leanblueprint checkdecls`, etc.). Trust nothing the report claims; check.
270	3. **Archive the report** to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` so the dashboard can render it. Use `cp` to copy, not move — the `task_results/` file stays so future iterations can find it.
271	4. **Update `STRATEGY.md`** if the findings changed the long-arc plan.
272	5. **Update `PROGRESS.md`** with whatever new prover objectives the output enables.
273	
274	### Canonical ordering
275	
276	When multiple subagents are needed in one iteration, the rough order is:
277	
278	1. Read-only reviewers / analogy-style precedent lookups — gather information first.
279	2. Structural / write-capable subagents (refactor-style) — make changes informed by what you learned.
280	3. Envelope / verification subagents — lock in the resulting definitions.
281	4. **Write prover objectives** — only after the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
282	
283	You may invoke each subagent multiple times in one iteration if justified. Each call needs a distinct slug.
284	
285	## Providing Informal Content to the Prover
286	
287	The prover performs significantly better when given rich informal mathematical guidance. Before assigning a task, you must ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof or proof sketch.
288	
289	**How to provide informal content:**
290	
291	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): Write directly in `PROGRESS.md` under the task objectives. Example: "Key idea: use Bolzano-Weierstrass to extract a convergent subsequence, then show the limit satisfies the property."
292	
293	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): Write as comments in the corresponding `.lean` file, above the declaration with `sorry`. Use `/- ... -/` block comments.
294	
295	- **Long content** (a full proof sketch, paper summary, or multi-step construction): Write in the relevant chapter `.tex` file in the blueprint. Reference the blueprint chapter in `PROGRESS.md` next to the objective.
296	
297	- **Other possibilities**: The above methods should be prioritized, but if relevant, you may use Web Search to find new references, or write a separate markdown file in the project (e.g. `informal_sketches/some_lemma.md`) and link to it from `PROGRESS.md`.
298	
299	**No matter which method you choose, always record in `PROGRESS.md`** where the informal content is located, so the prover can obtain it without searching.
300	
301	**All informal content must be mathematical, not syntactic.** Describe the proof in terms of mathematical objects, maps, and properties. Do not write Lean tactic sequences, term-mode proof expressions, or rewrite chains — those are the prover's output, not yours. If you find yourself writing `rw [← foo] ▸ bar.baz _`, stop and rephrase mathematically.
302	
303	**When the reference is vague** (e.g., "by Hiblot 1975" without proof details):
304	
305	1. Use `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` to generate an informal proof sketch from an external model.
306	2. Use Web Search to find the referenced paper and extract the key proof steps.
307	3. Write the result into a file and record the path in `PROGRESS.md`.
308	4. Do this **before** assigning the task to the prover — don't send the prover in blind.
309	
310	## Recognizing Prover Failure Modes
311	
312	### "Mathlib doesn't have it" — Missing Infrastructure
313	
314	The #1 failure mode. The prover reports that a sorry is unfillable because Mathlib lacks the infrastructure, then stops.
315	
316	**Your response:** This is YOUR job to solve, not the prover's. Never just pass it back with "try harder." You must actively find an alternative proof route:
317	
318	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`) — ask it: "Prove X without using [the missing infrastructure]. Only use tools available in Lean 4 Mathlib." Get a concrete alternative proof sketch.
319	2. **Use Web Search** — find the referenced paper or alternative proofs of the same result that avoid the missing infrastructure.
320	3. **Decompose differently** — break the problem into sub-lemmas where each sub-lemma only needs available infrastructure. The prover can implement Mathlib-level lemmas if you give it clear, self-contained goals.
321	4. **Check `mathlib-unavailable-theorems.md`** — if the missing infrastructure is in a known-unavailable domain, don't waste time looking for it. Focus on detours.
322	5. **If the infrastructure gap is in the definition itself** — invoke the refactor subagent to change the definition so it doesn't require the missing infrastructure downstream.
323	
324	Write the re-routed informal proof into the corresponding chapter `.tex` file (as a `\begin{proof} ... \end{proof}` body), then reassign the task to the prover. Do not reassign without providing an alternative in the chapter. The re-routed proof must be written mathematically — not as Lean syntax.
325	
326	### Wrong Construction — Building on a Flawed Foundation
327	
328	The prover chose a wrong construction and the sorry is mathematically unfillable, but the prover keeps trying instead of backtracking.
329	
330	**Your response:** If the fix is within a single file, instruct the prover to revert. If the fix requires cross-file changes, invoke the refactor subagent. Update the chapter `.tex` with the correct construction before the next prover round.
331	
332	### Not Using Web Search
333	
334	The prover searches only within Mathlib and gives up when it finds nothing, even when the blueprint references a specific paper.
335	
336	**Your response:** Explicitly instruct: "Use Web Search to find [paper name/arXiv ID], read the proof, decompose it into sub-lemmas, and formalize step by step." Update the chapter with the retrieved proof sketch.
337	
338	### Early Stopping on Hard Problems
339	
340	The prover stops and reports "done" when the remaining sorry requires significant effort.
341	
342	**Your response:** Reject the report. Break the hard problem into smaller sub-goals in the chapter `.tex` and assign them one at a time. Frame it as: "Formalize just sub-lemma L1 from the blueprint, then report back."
343	
344	### Using tricks (e.g. axioms, ad-hoc definitions, weakening hypotheses) to bypass hard parts
345	
346	The prover introduces new axioms or definitions that aren't in the blueprint to fill sorries, then reports completion. You should also never propose such tricks as a plan agent.
347	
348	**Your response:** Reject the report. Such tricks should not be accepted; they should be documented and then removed. You should then try to understand why this route was chosen and ensure that it will not be reproduced.
349	
350	### Repeated Blockers
351	
352	If task_results/ or review logs indicate that a prover has hit the exact same blocker for several consecutive iterations, you MUST escalate. Do not re-dispatch the lane with a slightly varied inline recipe. You might need to call a refactor subagent or rewrite the blueprint chapter.
353	
354	## Assessing Prover Progress
355	
356	### Three Indicators
357	
358	| Indicator | Meaning |
359	|-----------|---------|
360	| Sorry count (decreasing) | Direct progress — a sorry was filled |
361	| Code line count (increasing) | Infrastructure building — helpers, definitions |
362	| `\leanok` marks added | Prover confirmed formalization against the blueprint |
363	
364	Line count increasing + sorry count unchanged = the prover is building infrastructure. This is real progress.
365	
366	Line count unchanged + sorry count unchanged = zero progress.
367	
368	### Deep Stuck vs Early Abandonment
369	
370	| Pattern | Diagnosis | Response |
371	|---------|-----------|----------|
372	| 800+ lines, 2-3 sorries left | Deep stuck — needs math hint or infrastructure | Provide informal guidance via informal agent, suggest specific decomposition |
373	| <200 lines, sorry remaining | Early abandonment — prover gave up too quickly | Push harder: break into sub-goals, provide richer informal content |
374	
375	## Verification
376	
377	After a prover reports completion, always verify independently using only these two tools:
378	
379	1. Check sorry count: `${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/sorry_analyzer.py" <file> --format=summary`
380	2. Check compilation: `lean_diagnostic_messages(file)`
381	
382	Do not use `lean_run_code`, `lean_verify`, or any other Lean execution tool during verification — those are the prover's instruments. If `lean_diagnostic_messages` returns errors, report the error to the prover with context; do not attempt to diagnose or fix the Lean code yourself.
383	
384	3. Check axioms: confirm no new `axiom` declarations appear in the diff
385	4. Check blueprint consistency: `leanblueprint checkdecls` flags Lean names in the blueprint that don't exist. Run this after the prover has renamed or removed declarations.
386	
387	Never advance to the next stage based solely on the prover's word.
388	
389	## Dependency graph
390	
391	Before scoping objectives, run the bundled dependency-graph script instead of reconstructing the dependency map by hand. It parses every `.lean` file's imports plus every `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` for `\lean{…}` / `\uses{…}` / `\proves{…}` / `\leanok` / `\notready`, and emits a JSON view of the whole project in well under a second:
392	
393	```
394	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=json
395	```
396	
397	Use `--format=summary` for a one-screen overview, `--format=dot` if you need to share the graph elsewhere. Read this once per iteration to decide objective ordering — files with no upstream sorries should be formalised first, downstream files later.
398	
399	## Decomposition Strategy
400	
401	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem:
402	
403	1. Read the blueprint chapter to identify sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, L3, ...).
404	2. Read the files in `references/` related to it, if any, to ensure you understand and align with the original proof.
405	3. Check if the chapter is detailed enough — if not, expand it first (using informal agent / Web Search).
406	4. Assign one sub-lemma at a time: "Fill sorry for L1 only".
407	5. After L1 is done, verify, then assign L2.
408	6. Record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
409	
410	## Context Management
411	
412	Each prover iteration starts with fresh context. The prover does not remember previous iterations.
413	
414	- Provide **self-contained** objectives in `PROGRESS.md` — include all context the prover needs.
415	- Point the prover at its blueprint chapter — that is where the mathematical content lives.
416	- When a prover gets stuck on the same failure across multiple iterations, it is re-discovering the same dead end. Change the approach entirely — do not just repeat "try again".
417	- Document dead ends in `PROGRESS.md` so the prover doesn't repeat them.
418	
419	## Multi-Agent Coordination
420	
421	Provers run in parallel — one agent per file. Your objectives must be structured accordingly.
422	
423	### Writing objectives
424	
425	Number each objective clearly (1, 2, 3, ...). Each objective maps to **exactly one file**. Never assign two objectives to the same file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside.
426	
427	```markdown
428	## Current Objectives
429	
430	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorry in `filter_convergence` (line 156). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Core.tex` (see `thm:filter_convergence`).
431	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Measure.tex`. Use MeasureTheory.Measure.restrict_apply with finite spanning sets.
432	3. **`Topology.lean`** — Fill sorry in `compact_embedding` (line 203). Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Topology.tex`. Straightforward from CompactSpace + isClosedEmbedding.
433	```
434	
435	### Balancing difficulty
436	
437	Estimate the relative difficulty of each objective. If one file has significantly harder sorries than others, consider decomposing it into helper lemmas first (in a prior plan iteration) so the prover agent has smaller, more tractable goals. The goal is for all agents to finish around the same time.
438	
439	While you should balance difficulty, you should also aim at making concrete progress — therefore avoid giving shallow or trivial objectives.
440	
441	### Agent count
442	
443	- **Agent count = file count**: if 24 files need work, write 24 objectives — one per file. Do not artificially batch or limit the number of objectives. The shell script handles parallelism.
444	- If an experiment is restarted, check the compilation status of every target `.lean` file before planning. Prioritize files that still have `sorry` or compilation errors. Do not redo completed work.
445	
446	## Stage Transitions
447	
448	When all objectives in the current stage are met, advance `PROGRESS.md` to the next stage:
449	
450	- `autoformalize` → `prover` (when all statements are formalized)
451	- `prover` → `polish` (when all sorries are filled and verified)
452	- `polish` → `COMPLETE` (when proofs are clean and compile)

22	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` -- the Jacobian of a proper, smooth curve
23	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.ofCurve` -- the Abel-Jacobi map from a proper smooth curve to its
24	  Jacobian

30	* `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian.exists_unique_ofCurve_comp` -- the universal property of the Jacobian

59	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] : Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
60	  sorry

66	-- data

69	  sorry

9	import Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Smooth

16	/-! # Relative Kähler differentials for schemes
17	
18	This file constructs the sheaf of relative Kähler differentials `Ω_{X/S}`
19	of a morphism of schemes `f : X → S`, together with its universal derivation
20	`d : 𝒪_X → Ω_{X/S}`. It also states the cotangent exact sequence and the
21	characterisation of smoothness in terms of local freeness of `Ω`.
22	
23	The construction builds on Mathlib's ring-theoretic `KaehlerDifferential`
24	and the presheaf-of-modules differential construction
25	`PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials'`.
26	
27	## Status (iteration 064 — scaffold)
28	
29	All main declarations have `sorry` bodies. Closure trajectory is estimated
30	at ~10 iterations per `STRATEGY.md`.
31	
32	## References
33	
34	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Differentials.tex`.
35	-/
36	
37	set_option autoImplicit false
38	
39	universe u
40	
41	open CategoryTheory Limits TopologicalSpace AlgebraicGeometry
42	
43	namespace AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme
44	
45	variable {X Y S : Scheme.{u}}
46	
47	/-! ## The relative cotangent presheaf and sheaf -/
48	
49	/-- The relative cotangent **presheaf** of a morphism of schemes `f : X ⟶ S`.
50	
51	On each open `U ⊆ X`, the sections are the Kähler differential module of
52	the ring map `O_S(f(U)) → O_X(U)` induced by `f`. More precisely, we use
53	the inverse-image presheaf `f⁻¹ O_S` on `X` and the canonical map to `O_X`;
54	the Kähler differential construction then gives a presheaf of `O_X`-modules.
55	
56	Mathlib leverage: `TopCat.Presheaf.pullback` for `f⁻¹`,
57	`PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials'`
58	for the presheaf of Kähler differentials. -/
59	noncomputable def relativeDifferentialsPresheaf (f : X ⟶ S) : X.PresheafOfModules :=
60	  let φ' := ((TopCat.Presheaf.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction CommRingCat f.base).homEquiv _ _).symm
61	    f.c
62	  PresheafOfModules.DifferentialsConstruction.relativeDifferentials' φ'
63	
64	/-! ### Sheaf condition: decomposition
65	
66	Closing `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheaf` is the main outstanding
67	gap of Phase B step 1. The mathematical proof has three layers:
68	
69	1. **`KaehlerDifferential` commutes with localisation.** For a ring map
70	   `A → B` and `f ∈ B`, `Ω_{B[1/f]/A} ≅ Ω_{B/A} ⊗_B B[1/f]`. Mathlib's
71	   `KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule` (and the scheme variant
72	   `KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule_map`) provides this.
73	2. **The presheaf is a sheaf on the basis of affine basic opens.** On a
74	   basic open `D(f)` of `Spec B`, sections are `Ω_{B[1/f]/A}`. Gluing on a
75	   basic-open cover `{D(fᵢ)}` of `Spec B` follows from the localisation
76	   compatibility of step 1, applied to each `fᵢ`.
77	3. **Globalisation to all opens.** A presheaf on a scheme is a sheaf iff
78	   it is a sheaf on the basis of affine opens (more precisely, on the
79	   basic opens of every affine open). This is the standard scheme
80	   sheaf-of-modules construction.
81	
82	The substep theorems below are the natural decomposition. Each remains
83	`sorry` pending the full proof; the structure mirrors the blueprint at
84	`blueprint/src/chapters/Differentials.tex`. -/
85	
86	/-- Substep 1 (localisation compatibility): For an affine open `V` of `X`
87	mapped to an affine open `U` of `S`, the sections of
88	`relativeDifferentialsPresheaf` over `V` are isomorphic (as additive groups)
89	to the Kähler differential module `KaehlerDifferential (φ'.app (op V))`. -/
90	theorem relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_obj_kaehler (f : X ⟶ S)
91	    (V : (TopologicalSpace.Opens X.toTopCat)ᵒᵖ) :
92	    ((relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f).presheaf.obj V : Type _) =
93	      CommRingCat.KaehlerDifferential
94	        (((TopCat.Presheaf.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction CommRingCat
95	          f.base).homEquiv _ _).symm f.c |>.app V) :=
96	  rfl
97	
98	/-! ### Bar B scaffolding (iter-112): named sub-lemmas for Route (a)
99	
100	The Route (a) recipe (cf. `blueprint/src/chapters/Differentials.tex`
101	§ Sheaf condition for $\Omega_{X/S}$) factors the sheaf condition for
102	`relativeDifferentialsPresheaf` into three logically separate sub-claims,
103	each encapsulating one of the three steps:
104	
105	  Step 1 (forgetful reduction Ab ⥤ Type) — handled inline via
106	  `Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheaf_comp` with the underlying-types forgetful
107	  functor `forget AddCommGrpCat`.
108	
109	  Step 2 (affine-restricted sheaf condition; identification of
110	  `D(f) ↦ Ω_{B[1/f]/A}` with the tilde quasi-coherent sheaf
111	  `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde Ω_{B/A}` via
112	  `KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule_map`) — packaged as
113	  `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isLimit_affine_basis` (helper #1, body
114	  `sorry` pending Step 2 development).
115	
116	  Step 3 (globalisation by refining an arbitrary open cover through the
117	  affine basis and lifting the equalizer-products condition along
118	  cofinality, against `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafOpensLeCover`)
119	  — packaged as `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isLimit_opensLeCover`
120	  (helper #2, body `sorry` pending Step 3 development).
121	
122	Both helpers state the sheaf-of-types side of the condition (after the
123	Step 1 reduction); the main proof of
124	`relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheaf` then wires them together via the
125	chapter recipe. -/
126	
127	/-- **Bar B (iter-113) — Sub-helper A: unique-gluing form of the sheaf
128	condition.**
129	
130	The underlying type-presheaf of `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f`
131	satisfies the **unique-gluing** characterisation of the sheaf condition
132	(`TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafUniqueGluing` [verified]): for every family of
133	opens `U : ι → Opens X` and every compatible family of Kähler
134	differentials `sf i ∈ Ω_{X/S}(U i)`, there is a unique
135	`s ∈ Ω_{X/S}(iSup U)` whose restriction to `U i` equals `sf i` for all
136	`i`.
137	
138	This is the load-bearing mathematical content of helper #1 below: the
139	two are equivalent via the Mathlib chain
140	`isSheaf_of_isSheafUniqueGluing_types` [verified] +
141	`IsSheaf.isSheafOpensLeCover` [verified]. The unique-gluing form is
142	mathematically cleaner for Kähler differentials because it reduces to a
143	single existential about derivations on the supremum, directly
144	attackable via the universal property of `KaehlerDifferential` combined
145	with the gluing data already provided by the structure-sheaf-of-rings on
146	`X`.
147	
148	**Corrected recipe (iter-115, blueprint-aligned)** for closing the body:
149	
150	1. **Affine-basis identification.** The scheme `X` admits the canonical
151	   basis of affine opens (`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isBasis_affineOpens`
152	   [verified]). For each affine `V_α = Spec B_α` over `Spec A_α` and each
153	   basic open `D(g) ⊆ V_α`, the presheaf satisfies
154	   `F(D(g)) = Ω_{B_α[1/g]/A_α} ≅ Ω_{B_α/A_α} ⊗_{B_α} B_α[1/g]`, where
155	   the isomorphism is the canonical localisation iso of Kähler
156	   differentials supplied by `KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule`
157	   [verified, **no `_map` suffix**]
158	   (`Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.TensorProduct`). Under this
159	   identification, the restriction of `F` to the basis of basic opens of
160	   `V_α` matches the basis-restriction of `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde`
161	   [verified, **namespace `AlgebraicGeometry`, not
162	   `AlgebraicGeometry.Modules`**] applied to `Ω_{B_α/A_α}` on
163	   `V_α = Spec B_α`. The `isSheaf` field of `tilde` provides the sheaf
164	   property internal to each affine chart.
165	
166	2. **Hand-rolled cofinality descent.** For the family
167	   `U : ι → Opens X` refine to a basis-indexed cover
168	   `U' : ι' → Opens X` by intersecting each `U i` with the affine basis.
169	   By Step 1 the family `(sf i)` pulls back along the refinement to a
170	   compatible family on `U'` lying entirely on basic opens of affine
171	   charts; by the `tilde`-sheaf property on each affine chart this
172	   produces a unique gluing on `iSup_j U'_j = iSup_i U_i`. The basis-to-X
173	   bridge is **hand-rolled inline** — no off-the-shelf Mathlib lemma
174	   packages `Scheme.PresheafOfModules`-sheaf-on-affine-basis ⇒ sheaf
175	   on `X` (verified by mathlib-analogist-iter114; see
176	   `analogies/affine-basis-sheaf-bridge.md`). The structural target is
177	   `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafOpensLeCover` [verified] +
178	   `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing_types` [verified].
179	
180	3. **Uniqueness.** If `s, s' ∈ F(iSup U)` both restrict to `sf_i` on
181	   each `U_i`, then `s - s'` restricts to zero on each `U_i`; by Step 1
182	   it restricts to zero on each basic open `D(g) ⊆ U'_j`; by the
183	   `tilde`-sheaf property on each affine chart, `s - s' = 0` on the
184	   supremum. Equivalently, run through
185	   `TopCat.Presheaf.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq` [verified] applied to the
186	   structure sheaf, reduced to spanning generators via
187	   `KaehlerDifferential.span_range_derivation` [verified].
188	
189	The body remains a `sorry` (iter-115 PARTIAL); the recipe above is the
190	authoritative source-of-truth for the next prover round. -/
191	lemma relativeDifferentialsPresheaf_isSheafUniqueGluing_type (f : X ⟶ S) :
192	    TopCat.Presheaf.IsSheafUniqueGluing
193	      ((relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f).presheaf ⋙
194	        CategoryTheory.forget AddCommGrpCat) := by
195	  -- **Iter-115 structural opening (PARTIAL — recipe gap inline).**
196	  --
197	  -- After `intro ι U sf hcomp` the goal is an existence-and-uniqueness
198	  -- statement
199	  --   `∃! s ∈ F(iSup U), ∀ i, F.map (Opens.leSupr U i).op s = sf i`,
200	  -- where `F := (relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f).presheaf ⋙ forget AddCommGrpCat`.
201	  --
202	  -- Closing this goal requires the full 3-step recipe in the docstring
203	  -- above (Step 1 / Step 2 / Step 3). All three steps live on the
204	  -- **basis-to-X bridge** that the iter-114 mathlib-analogist verified is
205	  -- genuinely missing from Mathlib b80f227 (persistent file
206	  -- `analogies/affine-basis-sheaf-bridge.md`).
207	  --
208	  -- Concretely the missing inline construction is the hand-rolled
209	  -- cofinality descent of Step 2: refine `U` through the affine basis,
210	  -- use the `tilde`-sheaf property on each affine chart, and transfer
211	  -- the equalizer-products condition back to `U` along
212	  -- cofinality in the `OpensLeCover`-comma category over `iSup U`.
213	  -- See `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafOpensLeCover` [verified] +
214	  -- `TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafUniqueGluing_types` [verified]
215	  -- for the four-fold equivalent characterisation that anchors the
216	  -- descent.
217	  --
218	  -- Per the iter-115 plan's hard rules ("no further reformulation
219	  -- rounds" + Soundness rule against universally-false helper
220	  -- signatures), the inline `sorry` is left at this use-site rather than
221	  -- displaced through a sorry-bodied sub-helper. The structural
222	  -- `intro` advance below exposes the existential/uniqueness pair as
223	  -- separate obligations, so the next prover round can target each
224	  -- with the blueprint Steps 1–3 directly.
225	  intro ι U sf hcomp
226	  -- Goal:
227	  --   ⊢ ∃! s : F(iSup U), TopCat.Presheaf.IsGluing F U sf s
228	  --
229	  -- The witness `s` (existence half) is the output of Step 2's
230	  -- cofinality descent applied to `sf`. The uniqueness half follows
231	  -- from Step 3 (`eq_of_locally_eq` on the structure sheaf reduced to
232	  -- spanning generators via `KaehlerDifferential.span_range_derivation`).
233	  sorry
234	
235	/-- **Bar B helper #1 — Step 2+3 packaged at the type level
236	(load-bearing).**
237	
238	The underlying type-valued presheaf of `relativeDifferentialsPresheaf f`
239	(obtained by composing with `forget AddCommGrpCat`) satisfies the
240	`IsSheafOpensLeCover` sheaf condition: for every family
241	`U : ι → Opens X`, the cone `opensLeCoverCocone U` is sent to a limit.
242	
243	This single load-bearing claim packages both Step 2 (affine-chart
244	identification of `D(g) ↦ Ω_{B[1/g]/A}` with the tilde quasi-coherent
245	sheaf `AlgebraicGeometry.tilde Ω_{B/A}` via
246	`KaehlerDifferential.isLocalizedModule_map` [verified]) and Step 3
247	(globalisation across the affine cover provided by
248	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.isBasis_affineOpens` [verified], via the
249	refinement-and-cofinality argument against
250	`TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafOpensLeCover` [verified]).
